# The Future of Humanities in STEM Education: Integrating Soft Skills in Engineering

![The Future of Humanities in STEM Education: Integrating Soft Skills in Engineering](https://eitfaridabad.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Untitled-design.png)In the ever-evolving landscape of engineering and technology, the demand for professionals who possess not only technical expertise but also a well-rounded set of soft skills is growing. As the world becomes more interconnected and complex, the integration of humanities into STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) education is becoming a vital trend. This approach, which blends technical knowledge with the insights provided by the humanities, is preparing future engineers to navigate the challenges of the 21st century with greater agility and effectiveness.

![The Future of Humanities in STEM Education: Integrating Soft Skills in Engineering](https://eitfaridabad.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Untitled-design.png)

### **Understanding the Shift: Why Humanities in STEM?**

Traditionally, engineering education has focused heavily on technical subjects like mathematics, physics, and computer science. While these disciplines remain crucial, there’s a growing recognition that engineers also need skills that fall outside the typical STEM curriculum. These skills, often referred to as “soft skills,” include communication, critical thinking, teamwork, and ethical reasoning. Humanities courses, which encompass subjects like philosophy, sociology, and literature, provide the ideal platform for developing these abilities.

One of the [key benefits of integrating humanities into STEM education](https://eitfaridabad.com/applied-sciences-humanities/) is the improvement of communication skills. Engineers often work in teams, and they need to communicate complex ideas clearly and effectively to colleagues, clients, and stakeholders from diverse backgrounds. Courses in humanities, particularly those in writing, linguistics, and rhetoric, help students develop the ability to articulate their thoughts, both in writing and verbally. This is especially important in global projects where cross-cultural communication is crucial.

Humanities subjects encourage students to think critically and analyze problems from multiple perspectives. In engineering, this skill is invaluable. Engineers are frequently tasked with solving complex problems that require innovative solutions. Exposure to humanities fosters a mindset that looks beyond the technical aspects, considering the social, ethical, and environmental implications of engineering decisions. For example, a course in philosophy might challenge students to think about the ethical dimensions of artificial intelligence or the societal impact of automation.

As technology advances, ethical dilemmas in engineering are becoming more prevalent. Whether it’s the development of AI, the use of big data, or the environmental impact of engineering projects, engineers must navigate these issues with a strong ethical compass. Humanities courses, particularly those in ethics and philosophy, provide students with frameworks to approach these challenges thoughtfully. Understanding the broader implications of their work helps engineers make decisions that are not only technically sound but also ethically responsible.

Innovation often arises from the intersection of different fields. By studying the humanities, engineering students are exposed to diverse ways of thinking and problem-solving. This interdisciplinary approach can spark creativity, leading to innovative solutions that might not emerge from a purely technical education. For instance, a background in cultural studies can inspire engineers to design products that are more user-friendly and culturally sensitive, enhancing their appeal in global markets.

Leadership and teamwork are essential components of engineering projects. Humanities courses that emphasize collaboration, such as group projects in sociology or history, teach students how to work effectively in teams. They learn to lead, manage conflicts, and understand different viewpoints—skills that are crucial for engineers who often find themselves in leadership roles within multidisciplinary teams.

### **The Growing Trend: How Universities Are Adapting**

Recognizing the importance of these skills, many universities are now incorporating humanities courses into their engineering programs. Some institutions have introduced minors in areas like philosophy or ethics for engineering students, while others have integrated humanities modules into existing STEM courses. This trend is not just about adding more courses to the curriculum; it’s about creating a more holistic educational experience that prepares students for the complexities of the modern world.

Some universities are developing interdisciplinary programs that combine engineering with humanities. These programs allow students to major in engineering while taking a significant number of humanities courses, giving them a broader perspective on their field. For example, a program might combine computer science with courses in digital ethics, preparing students to address the ethical challenges in the tech industry.

Another approach is to incorporate humanities into capstone projects, where students work on real-world engineering problems. By requiring students to consider the ethical, social, and cultural aspects of their projects, these programs ensure that graduates are not only technically proficient but also socially aware and responsible.

### **The Benefits for Engineering Students**

The integration of humanities into STEM education is not just a trend—it’s a necessary evolution that benefits engineering students in numerous ways. By developing soft skills alongside technical expertise, students are better prepared to succeed in a rapidly changing world.

Employers today are looking for engineers who can do more than just perform technical tasks. They want professionals who can lead teams, communicate effectively, and think critically about the broader impact of their work. Graduates who have been trained in both STEM and humanities are often more attractive to employers because they bring a well-rounded skill set to the table.

The future of work is uncertain, with rapid technological advancements continually reshaping industries. Engineers with a background in humanities are more adaptable because they have been trained to think broadly and creatively. This adaptability makes them better equipped to handle the challenges of the future, whether they involve new technologies, ethical dilemmas, or global challenges.

Finally, integrating humanities into STEM education helps students find greater purpose in their work. By understanding the social and ethical implications of their work, engineers can contribute to society in meaningful ways. This sense of purpose not only enhances job satisfaction but also drives innovation and excellence in their careers.

### **Conclusion**

The future of engineering lies in a more integrated approach to education—one that combines the strengths of STEM with the insights of the humanities. As universities continue to adopt this model, we can expect to see a new generation of engineers who are not only technically skilled but also equipped with the soft skills needed to navigate the complexities of the modern world. These well-rounded professionals will be the leaders, innovators, and problem-solvers of tomorrow, driving progress in ways that are ethical, responsible, and inclusive.

By embracing the humanities, STEM education is evolving to meet the demands of the future, preparing engineers to make a positive impact on the world.

![Image 2: Concept of diversity of skills with silhouettes of people with different brains (e.g., math, science, art, music, etc.)](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1330731514-800x500.jpg)
Combining STEM and Humanities: Broaden skills and enrich learning
=================================================================

[January 10, 2025](https://citl.news.niu.edu/2025/01/10/combining-stem-and-humanities-broaden-skills-and-enrich-learning/ "9:35 am")[Amanda Hirsch](https://citl.news.niu.edu/author/a1404391/ "View all posts by Amanda Hirsch")[Resources](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/resources/), [Teaching](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/teaching/), [Tips](https://citl.news.niu.edu/category/tips/)

STEM majors might kick off their careers with higher salaries, but research shows that humanities majors often catch up by mid-career, thanks to their adaptability and critical thinking skills—qualities that industries are valuing more and more these days (Goldfine, 2024). This highlights the growing need for interdisciplinary approaches to tackle complex problems that require creative, out-of-the-box solutions. Bringing STEM and the humanities together doesn’t just expand students’ skill sets—it also makes learning more enriching and prepares them for lasting success, both professionally and personally.

The Value of Interdisciplinary Learning
=======================================

![Image 3: Left right human brain concept. Creative art brain side (right) explodes with paint splatter; and mathematical brain side (left) with formulas.](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-2018098760-300x169.jpg)Student complaints about non-STEM general education courses are both misguided and short-sighted—STEM and humanities aren’t opposites; they actually complement each other (Bourdeau & Wood, 2019). One promising solution is “Humanistic STEM,” which blends scientific and technical studies with an emphasis on human welfare, values, and culture (Bourdeau & Wood, 2019; Bourdeau & Wood, 2021). This approach meets the growing demand for STEM graduates who are not only technically skilled but also creative, empathetic, ethical, and globally aware. Unlike STEAM, which typically emphasizes the arts, Humanistic STEM (H-STEM) prioritizes interdisciplinary collaboration and integration of perspectives across STEM and humanities (Bourdeau & Wood, 2021). Humanities courses encourage students to engage with nuanced issues and explore diverse perspectives, providing transformative learning experiences (MIT Open Learning, 2024). By studying both humanities and STEM, students can become well-rounded and globally aware graduates who think criticallyare ready to solve tomorrow’s toughest challenges with both technical expertise and ethical insight.

Humanities Complements STEM
===========================

Studying subjects like literature, history, and philosophy helps students build skills—like empathy, creativity, critical thinking, communication, and adaptability—that they will use throughout their careers (Goldfine, 2024; Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2019). Abilities such as writing, oral communication, problem-solving, teamwork, ethical decision-making, sociopolitical reasoning, and leadership are critical for success and advancement in today’s ever-changing workplaces (Goldfine, 2024; Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2019; MIT Open Learning, 2024). Humanities studies also spark curiosity, empathy, and global sensibility, while encouraging self-expression and helping students to feel connected to the broader world (Goldfine, 2024). They push students to think critically about abstract ideas and approach challenges from fresh perspectives, boosting their ability to solve complex issues in creative and innovative ways (Goldfine, 2024).

![Image 4: Woman standing at podium with laptop giving a speech or presentation. ](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1348871022-300x191.jpg)Storytelling, for example, plays an important role in making technical work impactful. Whether crafting compelling grant proposals and journal articles, or communicating ideas in public and professional settings, being able to frame technical work within broader societal narratives is the key to advancing STEM research (Koblitz, 2017). Humanities disciplines like literature, history, and world languages can help STEM professionals develop the skill of storytelling, enabling them to link their work to the human experience and drive meaningful change. Humanities also contribute to STEM in practical ways, such as:

*   Writing clear and persuasive scholarly articles or funding proposals
*   Navigating ethical minefields in technology and AI
*   Enhancing teamwork and leadership through empathy and effective communication

When STEM and humanities come together, students gain versatile skills that strengthen technical abilities while preparing them to approach challenges holistically and through a socially conscious lens.

STEM also Strengthens Humanities
================================

![Image 5: Sculpture Thinker With Golden VR Glasses On Purple Background](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1204290480-300x191.jpg)Humanities doesn’t just benefit STEM—the relationship works both ways. Bringing STEM into humanities studies allows students to blend technical skills with critical and creative thinking. For example, data analysis and visualization can help students dive deeper into historical trends, linguistic patterns, or cultural shifts. STEM tools also open the door to exciting digital projects like 3D modeling of historical artifacts, virtual reality experiences for cultural studies, or AI-assisted translation. These tools can even enhance storytelling with interactive timelines or digital narratives. Additionally, STEM can introduce humanities students to ethical dilemmas in technology, such as artificial intelligence and climate change, equipping them for interdisciplinary career opportunities where technical and ethical insights go hand in hand.

Challenges and Misconceptions
=============================

Some common misconceptions about integrating STEM and humanities include beliefs that “Humanities aren’t relevant to technical careers” and “STEM and humanities require separate skill sets.” These beliefs overlook how essential humanities are for fostering critical skills that STEM fields rely on, like communication, ethical reasoning, and creative problem-solving. While addressing these misconceptions is a key step, the bigger challenge lies in the logistics of creating and sustaining interdisciplinary programs.

![Image 6: Close-up of team of people brainstorming while analyzing mind map laid out on a table.](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-1358014160-300x200.jpg)Building successful STEM-humanities programs requires collaboration between faculty, administrators, and other stakeholders. For example, team-teaching logistics require careful coordination to balance workloads and ensure meaningful engagement between disciplines. Developing flexible, innovative curricula also demands ongoing creativity and adaptability from educators. These efforts are only sustainable with continual budgetary support and institutional commitment to provide the resources and incentives needed for cross-disciplinary collaboration (Cheema, 2022; Bourdeau & Wood, 2021).

Challenges like budget constraints, rigid or insular departmental structures, and differing visions among faculty can hinder progress. However, potential solutions like encouraging team teaching to share the workload and foster cross-disciplinary collaboration, and creating flexible curricula to integrate STEM and humanities concepts can make a big difference. Ultimately, institutions need to foster a culture of innovation that values interdisciplinary programs and recognizes their long-term value in preparing students for a rapidly changing and increasingly interconnected world.

The Future of Humanistic STEM
=============================

![Image 7: Hologram of the Brain and Statue of Goddess Themis. ](https://citl.news.niu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/14/2025/01/iStock-2149868673-300x165.jpg)The future of Humanistic STEM lies in its potential to address global challenges, such as the rise of AI, climate change, and public health crises, by combining the critical and ethical insights of the humanities with the technical expertise of STEM. As technology continues to reshape industries and society, interdisciplinary education equips students with adaptability, communication, and critical thinking skills, essential qualities in an ever-shifting professional landscape. Investing in humanities and STEM together not only enhances students’ career prospects but also fosters personal growth, enabling students to connect their work to broader societal narratives and make meaningful contributions. Educators and institutions should champion interdisciplinary approaches to prepare students for the complex challenges of today and tomorrow. As faculty, you can start by exploring and advocating for interdisciplinary programs to embrace the intersection of STEM and humanities in your own discipline.

References
==========

*   [Humanistic STEM: From Concept to Course](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/348947720_Humanistic_STEM_From_Concept_to_Course) (Bourdeau & Wood, 2021, _Journal of Humanistic Mathematics_)
*   [Integrating STEM & Humanities in Higher Ed](https://education-new-frontiers.com/2022/01/26/integrating-stem-humanities-in-higher-ed/) (Cheema, 2022, _Education New Frontiers_)
*   [Measuring the impact of humanities on STEM-focused education](https://openlearning.mit.edu/news/measuring-impact-humanities-stem-focused-education) (MIT Open Learning, 2024)
*   [What Is Humanistic STEM and Why Do We Need It?](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330768884_What_Is_Humanistic_STEM_and_Why_Do_We_Need_It#fullTextFileContent) (Bourdeau & Wood, 2019, _Journal of Humanistic Mathematics_)
*   [Why the Humanities Are Good for Getting Ahead](https://www.bowdoin.edu/news/2024/08/why-the-humanities-are-good-for-getting-ahead.html) (Goldfine, 2024, Bowdoin College)
*   [Why STEM Majors Need the Humanities](https://www.chronicle.com/article/why-stem-majors-need-the-humanities/) (Koblitz, 2017, _The Chronicle of Higher Education_)

# The Importance of Humanities Majors in a STEM-Driven Society

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20696%20522'%3E%3C/svg%3E "image")

Alice Dehghanzadeh

Senior Staff Writer

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20768'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

In an overwhelmingly STEM-driven society, humanities majors face constant scrutiny. The value for technological advancement is increasing ever so rapidly, often leaving the humanities in the dust. In the words of Robin Williams’ character in “Dead Poets Society,” “Medicine, law, business, [and] engineering” are all “necessary to sustain life,” while “poetry, beauty, romance, love … are what we stay alive for.”

Over winter break, a discourse suddenly emerged regarding the difference in humanities versus STEM majors’ importance and level of work on the UCSB YikYak, a popular community app. Hundreds of upvotes shone on each anonymous opinion. One user said that STEM majors “think they are superior,” while another described being non-STEM as “cringe.” Some claimed that it would be impossible for humanities majors to obtain jobs while others argued that the STEM field is oversaturated and therefore equally as difficult to find a job. It is clear that public opinion is divided, but one anonymous YikYak user captured a main point of the conversation: “You can be jobless with any degree. It’s about how you use your time in college to make connections and get internships — any major can fail at that.”

[Forbes Magazine acknowledges](https://www.forbes.com/sites/marvinkrislov/2023/11/10/why-the-humanities-matter-more-than-ever-even-when-you-keep-hearing-otherwise/) that college is expensive but that studying humanities is “every bit as important to future success in the workplace as learning to code, calculate, or raise capital.” The article goes on to explain how soft-skills, such as communication, emotional intelligence, and creativity are an essential appeal of an exceptional employee when compared to an average one. Said skills are honed by a classic liberal arts education, according to the article. Essentially, technical skills, while important, are only part of what is crucial in being an outstanding candidate.

One’s proficiency in the humanities is also a major testament to one’s ability to problem-solve and think critically, according to [The Center for Humanities and the Public Sphere](https://humanities.ufl.edu/archives/the-work-of-the-humanities-critical-thinking-in-life-and-labor/). The humanities test the limits of your mind to its greatest potential, forcing you to expand your knowledge and think beyond surface level. Through working with complex texts and historical events, students equip themselves with the necessary levels of creativity needed to work in and lead society. Decision-making is another vital asset to a humanities student, as it appropriately challenges them to look at several points of view and make informed judgments.

Ultimately, a humanities education aids students in becoming members of society that are well-versed in problem-solving.

The interconnection between STEM and humanities ideas is what is most challenging to achieve yet also most rewarding. In an ever-changing society, we need capable thinkers of both worlds to bridge the gap and work together holistically to produce innovative ideas.

Many of the world’s problems — inequality, discrimination, climate change, and political division, to name a few — are rooted in human behavior and ethics. To the humanities student, these issues provide the lens through which one can understand and analyze the intricacies of human beliefs and values. I believe that studying philosophy, literature, history, and other fields help humanities students comprehend societal challenges, because they provide the context to understanding the motivations and consequences of past events. With this knowledge, humanities students are better furnished with the ability to enact positive change. Humanities majors are not solely important in college but beyond, aiding in the foundation of a thoughtful and compassionate world.

STEM also plays an essential role in founding a modern society. The advancements in science and technology help drive society, and directly impact how we live. This progress cannot exist, however, without a comprehensive understanding of how the world works, which is where humanities come in, giving STEM the ethics and values to which they must learn to work with.

Both STEM and humanities fields remain crucial in providing students with the skills to live as thought-provoking and meaningful citizens of society. Instead of being polarized, students should come together to celebrate their differences in the fields they choose to study while acknowledging that one cannot live without the other. The potential for collaborative power is indefinite.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%2096%2096'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

# [How Humanities Courses Can Help You Get the Most Out of a STEM Education](/blog/how-humanities-courses-can-help-you-get-most-out-stem-education)

"What are you going to do with *that* degree?" is an all-too-familiar question asked of many liberal arts students, often in favor of the seemingly straightforward career paths offered by a science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) education. Although numerous reports of enrollment in humanities programs across the country have consistently fallen over time, the core concepts of this discipline offer practical skills that are crucial in professional settings, like critical thinking and clear communication. Discover the hidden value humanities courses add to academic and professional careers.

## The Benefits of an Evolved Curriculum

Humanities courses help teach STEM students how to think creatively, reason, and analyze situations. These skills aren't limited to teachers and philosophers—doctors, lawyers, business leaders, and engineers can all be more successful in their careers by studying a balanced curriculum. Though experts have argued over which areas fall within the humanities discipline and how exactly to define the parameters, it's been said that by studying the humanities, the student has the opportunity to get to know oneself and others better. Through studying language, behavior, the arts, and history, professionals can better connect with customers, clients, patients, and entire communities through their work.

![Teacher writing on whiteboard - Tulane School of Professional Advancement](/sites/default/files/styles/cke_media_resize_medium/public/2024-06/how-humanitites-course-can-help-you-get-the-most-out-of-a-stem-education.jpeg?itok=KHUscmua)

Though it may feel as though certain careers aren't exactly people-focused or rooted deeply in reading, writing, or business communication, these are far-reaching skills that can help professionals become more effective in consulting, reporting, and interacting with coworkers.

## What Can You Do With a Liberal Arts Education Anyway?

A standalone education in the humanities and [social sciences](https://sopa.tulane.edu/degrees-programs/bachelors-degrees/bachelor-arts-social-sciences) can prepare students to be successful in a variety of work settings. Humanities graduates may pursue careers in consulting, journalism, publishing, [marketing](https://sopa.tulane.edu/degrees-programs/bachelors-degrees/bachelor-arts-public-relations), sales, advertising, education, [non-profit management](https://sopa.tulane.edu/degrees-programs/certificates/graduate-certificates/graduate-certificate-nonprofit-and-strategic-philanthropy-management), [public service](https://sopa.tulane.edu/degrees-programs/masters-degrees/master-of-public-administration), research, and much more. These skills help professionals ask questions about the world and teach them how to approach and analyze complex, imperfect, and subjective information.

## Earn a Degree From a Nationally Recognized Institution

Tulane School of Professional Advancement boasts a renowned legacy of excellence in the humanities and liberal arts. [Our dedicated faculty](https://sopa.tulane.edu/degrees-programs/programs-study/humanities-social-sciences/faculty) is committed to supporting students throughout their journeys and helping them succeed beyond the classroom. [Apply online](https://sopa.tulane.edu/apply) to discover how you can elevate your career in STEM or the liberal arts with a degree from Tulane SoPA.

# Five Reasons STEM Students Should Study Humanities

If you're studying a STEM subject, you might feel that your university's humanities requirements are irrelevant and unnecessary. Why read Shakespeare when you're studying science or learn about politics if your a physics major? While the humanities and STEM subjects may seem completely unrelated, there are some very important reasons to diversify your studies. Here are five ways human sciences can help natural science students succeed.

![Five Reasons STEM Students Should Study Humanities](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/c_limit,w_1920/dpr_auto/f_auto/q_auto/v1/element/25/25528_1_View.jpg)

**Once upon a time, the humanities reigned supreme in the halls of higher education. Philosophy, literature, languages, and rhetoric were seen as the pinnacles of learning and the world's greatest minds were as comfortable with iambic pentameter as they were with the quadratic equation. But as society has shifted towards innovation and technological development, the human sciences are often neglected or disdained as soft subjects without practical application. But there's a reason [why the importance of humanities degrees are increasing](https://www.educations.com/article/Why-the-Humanities-Are-More-Important-Than-Ever/)– and it's not just because [rhyme and music make memorizing the periodic table a lot easier](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=r7hO-1ItqXw). We're not suggesting that students with STEM aspirations abandon the hard sciences, but here's why a minor in a liberal arts subject could provide the balance that's needed for future success.**

![Young woman looking in the distance](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/f_auto/q_auto/g_auto/w_auto/dpr_2.0/element/25/25528_1_View.jpg)

**1. Broaden your Career View**  
Science doesn't happen in a vacuum, and even in the most sterile lab environment outside forces will have a big impact on the direction of your research and your job prospects. It may not be ideal, but the reality of the scientific world is that research funding is often dictated by [politics](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Political-Science/), and scientific projects can be dependent on cultural shifts. Think about current events and the ways in which politicians leverage infectious diseases, climate science, and technological breakthroughs. And while science students often imagine careers in sterile, white laboratories, STEM subjects and the non-science world are constantly colliding. The recent Zika-virus outbreak in South America is a perfect example of the ways in which science, politics, social planning, and [marketing](http://www.mbastudies.com/article/Four-Reasons-Business-Students-Should-Study-the-Arts/) must work together. STEM professionals need to understand not just how the natural world works, but how they can apply their knowledge and skills to real-world issues.

![Shouting at a brick wallA businessman shouting at a brick wall which represents a barrier to his ability to reach potential clients.  The people & brick wall and the background are on separate labeled layers.](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/f_auto/q_auto/g_auto/w_auto/dpr_2.0/element/25/25529_2_Communicate.jpg)

**2. Communicate Clearly**  
Liberal arts won't just help you apply your scientific expertise to problems, they will help you communicate with the non-science world. Face it, scientific and technological research can be difficult to understand if you're not an expert. And just like Schrodinger's famously misinterpreted cat, lay-people often misunderstand the scientific world, sometimes with disastrous consequences. Learning to write clearly, to construct a rhetorical argument, and to simplify complex ideas are essential tools for the modern STEM professional.

![Male and female thinking together on th blackboard background](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/f_auto/q_auto/g_auto/w_auto/dpr_2.0/element/25/25527_3_Understand.jpg)

**3. Understand the World and its Inhabitants**  
Neuroscience may teach you how the human brain functions and physics promises that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. But people are not just electrical impulses and their reactions aren't always predictable. [Literature](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Literature/), poetry, [music](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Performing-Arts/Music/), and [art](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Visual-Arts/) are all expressions of individual feelings and studying them can help students understand both the world and the minds of other individuals. And, ironically enough, this may be a more important skill for a scientist than a [creative writing](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Creative-Writing/) student. Scientists must understand how their work will effect both the world and individuals in order to innovate.

![Postcard or background with a doll of Frankenstein,vintage colors.](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/f_auto/q_auto/g_auto/w_auto/dpr_2.0/element/25/25525_4_Ethics.jpg)

**4. Reinforce Cultural and Ethical Responsibility**  
But understanding the world isn't just about empathy and innovation. J. Robert Oppenheimer is famous for saying “Now I am become Death, destroyer of worlds” but the [physicist](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Physics/) was actually quoting from Hindu scripture. And as science and technology progress, it's important to constantly reassess the ethical and cultural impact of the development. Doing this requires a thorough understanding of the literary, cultural, [religious](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Theology/), and social influences that impact society. Reading Mary Shelley's Frankenstein isn't just an exercise in literary analysis and nineteenth century literature. The story questions the morality of scientific innovation and the responsibility of the scientist to both his creations and the world. Future scientists take note – the monster in Shelley's horror story isn't the creature.

![Multilingual languages and flags sign post against blue sky](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/f_auto/q_auto/g_auto/w_auto/dpr_2.0/element/25/25526_5_Languages.jpg)

**5. Foreign Languages are a Must!**  
As kindergarteners we learn that sharing is caring, and in the world of scientific and technological research, collaboration is key. This means that successful STEM professionals will often find themselves working in multi-cultural teams, cooperating with international facilities, or communicating across borders and boundaries. Scientists with foreign-language skills will find this multinational environment a lot easier to navigate. As you continue your studies, start considering your areas of concentration and the regions of the world where those industries are most common. [English](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Languages/English/), Chinese, and Spanish are the most prevalent languages in the world, but studying [French](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Languages/French/) or [German](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Languages/German/) will open doors in both technological and political arenas. Japanes, Korean, and Arabic are also smart choices. Don't forget [Latin and Greek](https://www.educations.com/Masters-Degree/Classical-studies/), which are vital to students of medicine and botany. But your language choice doesn't even need to be strategic – even [studying an obscure language](http://www.academiccourses.com/article/Four-Reasons-to-Study-a-Rare-Language/) can give your brain and your career a boost!

![](https://keystoneacademic-res.cloudinary.com/image/upload/c_limit,w_256/dpr_auto/f_auto/q_auto/v1/Alumni_profile_photos_3_zhokre.png)

##### Elizabeth Koprowski, PhD Author

Author

Elizabeth is a content campaign specialist at educations.com with more than 20 years of experience in international higher education and study abroad. Her background in travel writing and travel history helps guide her research and content creation. Elizabeth is committed to helping students worldwide find the right study abroad experience.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating humanities fosters essential soft skills like empathy and ethical reasoning, shaping engineers into thoughtful leaders who navigate complex societal challenges responsibly.
Humanities education equips STEM professionals to understand and tackle global issues like climate change and inequality, ensuring solutions are socially informed and ethically sound.
The intersection of humanities and STEM encourages creative problem-solving and interdisciplinary collaboration, leading to innovative solutions that address real-world problems effectively.
